sections.vendors: No new monthly supplier-list drop this week — H&M Group's supply-chain transparency page now points to the May 2026 spreadsheet and states the list is updated quarterly rather than monthly, so suppliers shouldn't expect a fresh xlsx again until closer to Q3/Q4. Latest published numbers: 554 commercial suppliers across 969 tier-1 factories, with China and Bangladesh remaining the largest production markets.
sections.strategy: The main dated news this week: ARKET will open its second physical Paris store in 2026, alongside its existing Rue des Archives flagship (announced 9 July). This continues the pattern from Q2 results (published 25 June, just outside this window) where H&M Group flagged a delayered, market-led organisation and cost cuts, while still investing selectively in premium-brand physical retail (ARKET, COS) even as core H&M store count shrinks (4,038 stores at end of May, down from 4,166 a year earlier).
soWhat: Supplier-list cadence has shifted from monthly to quarterly per H&M's own site — don't plan around a monthly refresh; the next real update likely lands with the Q3 report cycle (autumn 2026).

Bangladesh LDC graduation (November 2026) remains the single biggest sourcing-cost risk on the horizon — factories running large-scale, high-volume lines (H&M's Bangladesh base skews toward 1,000+ worker factories) are best positioned to absorb the 10–12% duty hit once the EU grace period narrows.

H&M Group's capital is visibly tilting toward COS/ARKET/& Other Stories store growth (Paris, Stockholm, Mexico expansions announced in recent weeks) even as core H&M's footprint contracts — suppliers serving the premium sub-brands may see comparatively steadier order flow than core H&M vendors this year.
